matter.title: Roe et al. v. Coastal Outfitters LLC et al.
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe and Maria Q. Fairbanks invested in a Series A preferred round of Coastal Outfitters LLC and now sue the company, its CEO Olivia D. Jessup, and (by third-party complaint) its outside auditor, alleging the offering misrepresented the status of a flagship contract.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe and Maria Q. Fairbanks invested in a Series A preferred round of Coastal Outfitters LLC and now sue the company, its CEO Olivia D. Jessup, and (by third-party complaint) its outside auditor, alleging the offering misrepresented the status of a flagship contract.
fact_pattern.narrative:
On or about 2023-06-03, Grace C. Roe and Maria Q. Fairbanks together committed roughly $3131282 to a Series A preferred round of Coastal Outfitters LLC, a closely-held Maine company led by its chief executive, Olivia D. Jessup. The investment was solicited on the strength of an offering package and audited financial statements.

The plaintiffs allege that the offering materials materially misrepresented the status of a flagship contract, that Olivia D. Jessup knew or recklessly disregarded the true facts, and that the audited statements prepared by Summit Logistics LLC masked the problem. The plaintiffs say they discovered the misstatements around 2024-11-03, after which the investment lost most of its value.

The plaintiffs filed suit in Sagadahoc County Superior Court seeking damages of approximately $1628106. The company answered and counterclaimed, asserted cross-claims against its CEO, and filed a third-party complaint impleading Summit Logistics LLC, which issued the audited financial statements relied upon in the offering. The matter is now in active, contested discovery.
undisputed_facts: The plaintiffs invested in Coastal Outfitters LLC on or about 2023-06-03.
Summit Logistics LLC audited the financial statements used in the offering.
The investment's value declined materially after 2024-11-03.
disputed_facts: Whether the offering materials misrepresented the status of a flagship contract.
Whether Olivia D. Jessup acted with scienter or merely made an honest business judgment.
Whether the auditor's work fell below professional standards and caused the loss.
The amount of recoverable damages and whether punitive damages are available.

third_parties.name: Pine Tree Community Bank
third_parties.relevance: Held the investment proceeds at closing; records trace the use of funds.

third_parties.name: Beatrice S. Danforth
third_parties.relevance: Covered the company and can speak to market expectations.

third_parties.name: Lighthouse Logistics LLC
third_parties.relevance: Marketed the securities to the plaintiffs.

third_parties.name: Beatrice M. Ellsworth
third_parties.relevance: Whistleblower who flagged the accounting irregularities before resigning.

exchanges.question: How did you come to invest, and how much?
exchanges.answer: We put in about $3131282 into a Series A preferred round. We relied on their offering deck and the audited financials. Maria and I went in together.

exchanges.question: When did you realize something was wrong?
exchanges.answer: Around 2024-11-03. The story they told about the status of a flagship contract turned out to be false, and a former finance executive confirmed it.

exchanges.question: What is your view of the auditor's role?
exchanges.answer: Their name was on the financials we relied on. If they had done their job, this would have been caught.

exchanges.question: What outcome do you want?
exchanges.answer: Recover what we lost, hold Olivia accountable, and get there efficiently. We will go to trial if we must, but a sensible settlement is fine.

exchanges.question: How do you feel about the cost and exposure of litigation?
exchanges.answer: We know it is expensive and they are fighting hard with counterclaims. We are committed but cost-conscious.

goals: Recover the plaintiffs' investment losses, approximately $1628106.
Establish liability of the CEO for breach of fiduciary duty and fraud.
Preserve the third-party claim against the auditor as an additional source of recovery.
priorities: Maximizing leverage for a favorable settlement.
desired_outcomes: A judgment or settlement compensating the plaintiffs' losses plus costs.
A finding (or strong settlement posture) on fiduciary breach and misrepresentation.
non_starters: A walk-away that leaves the misrepresentation unaddressed.

constraints.budget: Substantial but monitored; the plaintiffs want staged budgets per phase.
constraints.timeline: Wants the case trial-ready within roughly eighteen months of filing.

issues.issue: Whether the auditor is liable for negligent misrepresentation to non-client investors
Whether the CEO breached fiduciary duties owed to investors

expert_opinions.topic: Compliance with generally accepted auditing standards
expert_opinions.opinion: The audit conformed to professional standards given the information available.

expert_opinions.topic: Application of the business judgment rule
expert_opinions.opinion: The CEO's decisions fell within a reasonable range of business judgment.

evidence.title: Audited financial statements
evidence.description: The audited statements at the center of the dispute.

evidence.title: Escrow account statements
evidence.description: Records tracing the use of the investment proceeds.

evidence.title: Whistleblower memorandum
evidence.description: The former VP of Finance's internal memo flagging the issue.

evidence.title: Board meeting minutes
evidence.description: Minutes approving the projections and offering.

evidence.title: Auditor workpapers
evidence.description: The audit file produced in discovery.

evidence.title: Private offering memorandum
evidence.description: The offering deck and PPM relied upon by the plaintiffs.

amounts.label: Estimated plaintiff-side fees and costs through trial

elements: Breach of the duty of loyalty or care
A false statement in the course of business

causes_of_action.claim: Violation of the Maine Unfair Trade Practices Act
elements: In the conduct of trade or commerce
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Actual damages, attorney fees, and costs

affirmative_defenses.basis: The plaintiffs ratified the transaction by accepting later disclosures.
The complaint does not plead fraud with particularity. M.R. Civ. P. 9(b).
The plaintiffs were sophisticated and failed to conduct due diligence. 14 M.R.S. § 156.

counterclaims.relief: Damages and injunctive relief for disclosure of confidential information.
Damages for allegedly false public statements about the company.

cross_claims.basis: If the company is liable, the CEO must indemnify it for his own misconduct.

third_party_claims.basis: The auditor's negligent audit caused or contributed to any loss.

motions.title: Motion in limine to exclude the damages expert
motions.relief: Exclusion of the plaintiffs' damages model.

motions.title: Motion to amend to add a punitive damages claim
motions.relief: Leave to add a punitive damages demand.

motions.title: Motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim
motions.relief: Dismissal of the fraud and UTPA counts.

motions.title: Motion to compel production of audit workpapers
motions.relief: An order compelling the auditor's workpapers.

docket.entry: Complaint filed and served on the defendants.
Complaint and demand for jury trial filed.
Defendants' answer, counterclaims, and cross-claim filed.
Defendants served their Answer asserting affirmative defenses and a Counterclaim for breach of contract and tortious interference.
Third-party complaint against the auditor filed.
Scheduling order entered; discovery deadline set.
Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss Count III (negligent misrepresentation) under M.R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6).
Order denying the motion to dismiss.
Client reports the defendants are liquidating the disputed inventory and moving sale proceeds offshore, threatening to defeat any judgment.
Order compelling production of the audit workpapers.
Plaintiffs' expert reports served; discovery continues.

procedure.event: Complaint filed and served on the defendants.
Defendants served their Answer asserting affirmative defenses and a Counterclaim for breach of contract and tortious interference.
procedure.deadline_rule: M.R. Civ. P. 7(a) / 12(a) — reply to a counterclaim within 21 days of service.

procedure.event: Defendants filed a Motion to Dismiss Count III (negligent misrepresentation) under M.R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6).
procedure.deadline_rule: M.R. Civ. P. 7(b)(1)(A) — opposition to a motion within 21 days.

procedure.event: Client reports the defendants are liquidating the disputed inventory and moving sale proceeds offshore, threatening to defeat any judgment.
procedure.deadline_rule: M.R. Civ. P. 65 — motion for a temporary restraining order / preliminary injunction; attachment under M.R. Civ. P. 4A/4B.

facts.misrepresentation_topic: the status of a flagship contract
facts.auditor_role: issued the audited financial statements relied upon in the offering
